skill.name: azure-eventhub-ts
skill.description: Build TypeScript event streaming applications with Azure Event Hubs and the @azure/event-hubs SDK.
skill.summary: Guides TypeScript producers, consumers, checkpointing, partitions, and error handling for Azure Event Hubs.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static external command findings are false positives from Markdown code fences and benign npm installation examples. The skill is documentation-only and shows legitimate Azure Event Hubs usage, but it includes cloud network endpoints, environment variable reads for resource names, and examples that log event payloads.

low_findings.title: Benign Package Installation Commands
low_findings.description: The flagged external command locations are Markdown examples for npm package installation. They are not executed by the skill and do not include dynamic user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is inside bash code fences and only installs Azure SDK packages. I found no executable script file or command interpolation.

low_findings.title: Azure Cloud Endpoint Construction
low_findings.description: The skill shows how to construct an Azure Blob Storage URL for checkpoint storage. This is expected for Event Hubs checkpointing but involves outbound cloud access when users run derived code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint points to Azure Blob Storage using user-provided account and container names. This is legitimate SDK usage, not evidence of exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Reads for Resource Names
low_findings.description: The examples read EVENTHUB_NAMESPACE and EVENTHUB_NAME from process.env. These values identify Azure resources and are not credentials in the shown code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact variables are service identifiers used with DefaultAzureCredential. No secret, token, or connection string access is shown.

low_findings.title: Example Payload Logging
low_findings.description: Several examples print event bodies, properties, offsets, and errors. Users could expose sensitive telemetry if they copy this logging into production unchanged.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The logging calls are directly visible and include event bodies and metadata. The risk depends on the user data sent through Event Hubs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Backtick Findings Are Markdown Fence False Positives
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution findings, but the cited lines are Markdown code fences or TypeScript template literals in documentation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited locations shows documentation snippets only. No Ruby file, shell script, or executable skill hook exists.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production Code Should Avoid Raw Event Logging
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples log event content for demonstration. Production implementations should redact or omit sensitive fields before logging.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code prints event data directly. This is a common documentation shortcut, not malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Build Azure Event Hubs TypeScript Streams
content.value_statement: TypeScript teams need reliable patterns for high-throughput event ingestion and partitioned consumers. This skill provides Azure Event Hubs SDK examples for producers, consumers, checkpointing, and errors.
actual_capabilities: Shows Azure Event Hubs producer and consumer setup with TypeScript.
Explains DefaultAzureCredential authentication for Event Hubs clients.
Demonstrates batch sending, partition IDs, and partition keys.
Covers consumer subscriptions, start positions, and checkpoint storage.
Includes event properties, error handling, and client lifecycle guidance.
limitations: Does not provision Azure resources or configure cloud permissions.
Does not include a runnable project scaffold or automated tests.
Examples need adaptation for application-specific schemas and retry policies.
Logging examples should be changed before handling sensitive production data.

use_cases.title: Create Telemetry Ingestion
use_cases.description: Build a TypeScript producer that sends device or service events to Azure Event Hubs in batches.

use_cases.title: Process Event Streams Reliably
use_cases.description: Implement consumers with checkpointing so workers can resume after deployment, failures, or scaling events.

use_cases.title: Design Partitioned Pipelines
use_cases.description: Use partition keys and consumer groups to preserve ordering while supporting separate analytics workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Send My First Events
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a simple TypeScript Azure Event Hubs producer that sends three telemetry events in one batch.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Basic Consumer
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add a TypeScript consumer that reads from the default consumer group and handles processing errors.

prompt_templates.title: Add Checkpointing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to update my Event Hubs consumer with Azure Blob checkpoint storage and safe checkpoint timing.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Partition Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to propose partition keys, consumer groups, start positions, and monitoring points for a multi-tenant telemetry pipeline.

output_examples.input: Request: Build a producer for device telemetry.
output_examples.output: A TypeScript producer outline with Azure identity authentication, batch creation, event bodies, event properties, send flow, and client cleanup.
A consumer design that uses a subscription handler, blob checkpoint storage, error handling, and checkpoint updates after successful processing.
A recommendation to use stable partition keys per device or tenant, separate consumer groups per workload, and lag monitoring per partition.

best_practices: Use managed identity or DefaultAzureCredential instead of embedding connection strings.
Checkpoint only after events have been processed successfully.
Use partition keys for related events that require ordered processing.
anti_patterns: Do not log raw event bodies when telemetry can contain sensitive data.
Do not checkpoint failed batches that need retry or dead-letter handling.
Do not force all events into one partition without a throughput reason.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. It provides TypeScript SDK guidance. You must create Event Hubs, storage accounts, and permissions separately.

faq.question: Can it use managed identity?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples use DefaultAzureCredential, which supports managed identity in Azure-hosted environments.

faq.question: Does it cover checkpointing?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows BlobCheckpointStore usage with Azure Blob Storage for production consumers.

faq.question: Is it limited to IoT data?
faq.answer: No. It can guide event streaming for telemetry, application events, analytics, and event-driven systems.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It favors Azure identity authentication, but production teams should add least-privilege roles and careful logging.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
